text.content:
The mook pack was designed with a simple methodology in mind, where each gang has a couple rank-and-file members associated with it, a few elites, and a boss. These tiers of enemy should be self-evident when you review different sheets in the pack - but with the addition of the Difficulty Tier system, you can now accurately set up a scene to your benefit.

Here are some rules I had in mind when designing the pack.

  • Most mooks should be low-tier - Unless you deliberately want to create a high-stakes or elite-heavy situation where it wouldn't be the case, fill out your scenes with an abundance of low-tier enemies.

    • The included "generic" enemies are great for this - but of the pack's orgs also have at least 1 low-tier, "rank-and-file" member that can also be used to provide disposable bodies.

    • Doing this is useful for helping build the power fantasy of the setting - if you want a situation where the edgerunners feel like the main characters, use low-tier mooks to help sell that idea.

  • 3 - 2 - 1 Ratio - Generally, a challenging and involved/extended combat scene will involve 2 or 3 low-tier mooks for each PC, about a third of that amount of elites to help give orders, and, optionally, 1 or 2 high-tier enemies for truly dire encounters, to help drive home the threat, force a final confrontation or push a retreat.

  • There's A Hierarchy/Make Elites Feel Elite - Almost every org follows a conventional pyramid hierarchy - tougher guys towards the top giving orders, newer blood at the bottom doing what's necessary.

    • Don't just think of the higher tiers of enemies as a bit of extra meat for the grinder - they're the ones coordinating everything, and their presence (or absence) should affect how smart the lower-level mooks are!

Example Of Setting Up A Scene

Integrating mooks into a scene is simple.

  1. Set up your beat chart. The Core rules do a good job explaining this, though my scenes tend to usually encompass 2-3 "beats" at a time.

  2. Source your maps.

  3. Select your gang. Virtually anything in NC is covered, at this point, so you should be safe to start reviewing the rules of the gang you decide to use, instead of having to improvise.

  4. Is the scene going to be:

    • Easy/Noncombative: Stick to low-level mooks OR pick a boss or elite, duplicate the sheet and change their details as necessary.

    • Typical: Distribute a scattering of low level (tier 2-3) mooks at a 2-or-3 to 1 ratio to player characters, and about a third of that amount in elite mooks (tier 4).

      • OR, if you prefer a smaller number of enemies, distribute a number of Tier 3-4 enemeis about equal to the number of PCs in the scene.

    • Hard: Distribute enemies for a typical scene, but add a boss or tier X challenge, OR double the number of elite mooks that are present.

  5. Determine loadouts - many mooks have several available loadout options. I recommend an even distribution of the options… or you can roll a die for each mook and pick their option at random.

It's that simple.

Each mook has a tier of power associated with it. The definition of those tiers is as follows:

Tier 1: Peon

The meanest of Night City and what happens when your average civilian is forced to put anything on the line.

  • Recommended Amount: 3 or more in a scene may pose a challenge to a single new edgerunner if they're isolated - but even then, most should be fine.

  • Typical Loadout: 5 or less SP, poor quality weapons, roll results average 12 or less.

  • Use Case: Fill out improvised scenes where there are no planned enemies of any consequence, like in bars or storefronts. Human clutter for the day-to-day in Night City.

  • Example: The peon rank is reserved exclusively for low-level (tier 1) generic street thugs, and civilians.

Tier 2: Troublemaker

The low-level gangoons and enforcers that fill Night City's streets, trying to make a name for themselves. Not especially threatening, but they outnumber every other threat in the city 10:1.

  • Recommended Amount: For most parties, can be placed in scenes at a 2:1 ratio to player characters for a threatening but non-lethal challenge.

  • Typical Loadout: 4-8 SP, poor quality weapons, roll results average around 14-15.

  • Use Case: Use to fill out the ranks of major organizations and encounters within NC as readily available, disposable bodies that won't be named or missed, most of the time.

  • Example: Generic Tier 2 Gangsters and RatSec Grunts, who can be found working for virtually every gang & corp in the city.

Tier 3: Rank-And-File

Low-level but accredited members of NC's various groups, with enough authority to wear the org's colors but not enough to pose a real threat on their own.

  • Recommended Amount: Should accompany lower-tier mooks in a scene at around or just above a 1:1 ratio with player characters, or be placed at a 2:1 ratio on their own for a modest threat which could be lethal if the dice go the wrong way for too long.

  • Typical Loadout: 9-12 SP, standard quality weapons, roll results for intended uses average around 16 but lower for outside-the-box actions.

  • Use Case: Use to provide thematic color to encounters without overwhelming players and show concrete, distinct presence of an associated organization within a scene.

  • Example: RatSec Elites and Execs, Militech Suppressors, Scav Savages, NCPD Patrol Officers.

Tier 4: Elite Threat

Mid-level, named threats, who run the bulk of activity for most orgs within NC. If a PC didn't choose to become an edgerunner, they probably would've eventually ended up in a position like this instead.

  • Recommended Amount: Should be outnumbered by player characters at around a 2:1 ratio; can be a credible threat to isolated players in 1-on-1 combat.

  • Typical Loadout: 11-13 SP, standard quality weapons, roll results trend towards a consistent 17-19.

  • Use Case: Use to accent scenes and provide leadership to lower-level mooks, or to interact with player character actions directly.

  • Example: Militech Specialists, Scav Techs & Monsters, NCPD SWAT Officers.

Tier 5: Org Boss

High-level named threats who hold significant hard power, in charge of leading the various orgs found around NC.

  • Recommended Amount: Use sparingly as climactic encounters within sessions - 1 or 2 total, hidden behind layers of lower-level mooks.

  • Typical Loadout: Minimum 11 SP, but often considerably higher if combat-oriented, often with specialized, expensive, and dangerous gear.

  • Use Case: Use as named opposition leaders in missions, forces to be appeased or else planned around, or as serious, high-level lieutenants of the organization in combat encounters.

  • Example: Militech Strike Commander, Bozo Joker, 6th Street Commander.

Tier X: Isolated Use

Some mooks don't fit cleanly into any of the above categories and are meant to be used to develop very particular scenes or encounters, such as members of the Iron Sights, or Arasaka or Scott Brown Real Estate agents. These mooks will have an X-Y designation (for example, Arasaka agents are X-5 Isolated Boss Threats) in their description, indicating they should be reserved for special cases.

  • Recommended Amount: 1-2 in a scene, regardless of party size.

  • Typical Loadout: Varies widely, but always competent and usually quite specialized. Expect a minimum of light armorjack and standard-quality weapons, with roll results averaging 17-19, but can be considerably better under various circumstances.

  • Use Case: Use as isolated agents to frame particular, customized beats - such as an assassin hidden among the civilians, or a special agent lurking in the shadows.

  • Example: Militech Brute/Demolitionist, Arasaka Agents

Thank you for downloading the mook pack! This expansion for Cyberpunk RED is designed to provide you an evergreen starting point for coloring your scenes, by producing a varied array of enemies to encompass most known organizations within NC during the Red Decades. Accompanying them are a variety of homebrew items and rules tweaks to accentuate and expand on the variety of playstyles which are available in the core rules - all of which are optionsl, but recommended in most cases.

Ready to get started? Here's what's first.

  1. Read the remainder of this journal, which has been reworked from earlier editions of the pack. The following pages will fully explain the contents of the pack, including how to use the generic mook skills, their abilities, difficulty tiers, the extra gear and mecchanics included beyond the homebrew rules tweaks, and even give general guidelines for setting up scenes with enemies from the pack.

  2. Read through @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsOther.uTvChMzbsBogllYG]{1. Night City Gangs & Corps - Associated Homebrew}, which contains a modest, well-tested list of recommended gear tweaks and new systems for use within the pack.

  3. Review the NC G&C Items compendiium (also explained in @UUID[.xXVYa2Vdn3SU6Oy8]{Items And Abilities}), which contains a wide array of loot additions and substitutes for your time within NC.

  4. Review @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsOther.JournalEntry.LBPq2LypcdwXi0Oz]{1a. Lawmen: Reloaded} and @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsOther.JournalEntry.y29RedVzrt0tExeY]{1b. Netrunning: Rebooted 2.0}, which contain pack-compatible rules for the lawman and netrunner, respectively.

    • Lawmen: Reloaded provides a needed buff to the Lawman's functionality, but more importantly, provides a distinct alternative to the mooks provided in the core rules.

      • Due to R. Talsorian's homebrew policy, officially-printed mook statblocks cannot be reproduced for redistribution outside the core rules, making them a hassle to enable. These alternative rules allow for the role's integration with the mook pack.

    • Netrunning: Rebooted 2.0 provides a simplified, slimmed down version of Netrunning, which no longer requires you reproduce the various demons and ICE found within the core rules for its use.

      • While Foundry RED provides a robust and automated means of generating NET infrastructures, similar to Lawmen, the team is unable to directly reproduce/distribute some components of the involved rules - the reboot provides a means of quick setup for play beyond those restrictions.

      • The Items compendium provides a suite of updated programs and other gear to help enable this alternate approach to Netrunning.

  5. Discuss which of the above elements you'd like to utilize in your games with your players.

  6. Get started running your game, and have fun!

Enjoy.

For simplicity and ease of use, Mook sheets have been simplified far beyond their original design. Each mook now only has 4 relevant stats + HP + Rep, and 4 relevant skills. (Note: included sheets are slightly more detailed for use in FoundryVTT.)

Stats: BODY, MOVE, REF, DEX, and HP. All work as you would expect.

Skills:

  • Lowdown for checks based in COOL, EMP, or WILL.

  • Martial for attack checks. Add REF and/or DEX to this score as appropriate.

    • Foundry automatically does this and there's no easy way to turn it off, hence the distinction. No action should be required from you when rolling to do so.

  • Physical for checks based in BODY, REF, or DEX which are not covered by the Martial skill, and for evasion.

    • If an enemy is able to dodge bullets, this will be specified in their ability description, as well as Like for most other checks, you won’t add any modifiers to the physical skill, if doing so.

  • Technical for specialized skills (Driving, play instrument, etc.) or checks based in INT or TECH.

  • Rep & Facedowns: Rep is determined by the gang's overall clout and presence; when making a facedown, calculate as REP + 1/2 lowdown (rounded down) + 1d10.

A reminder that these stats are made to be flexible and easy to expand on, so add bonuses or detriments to enemies while rolling in a scene as you feel is appropriate. It’s still gonna be easier than having to look up stats, and it’s a hell of a lot easier to design.

Some mooks make use of custom items. All mooks have custom abilities. These are explained below.

Abilities

Within the strict confines of the core rules, enemy identities in RED can become stale or repeitive very quickly. Simply put, there really going to be that much difference, between a member of 6th Street, for example, or an equivelent member of the Tyger Claws, in terms of stats and gear..

Abilities in the mook pack are intended to fix this problem, providing the pack additional design tools for producing unique encounters. They come in two varieties, and can be found on the sheet of each mook, as follows:

  • Gang/Corp-Wide Abilities are wide ranging general effects which are granted to every mook who's a part of the organization. These are usually passive effects which provide mooks an advantage in areas where the corp would be efficient.

    • For example, Militech's rigerous drills and training allow similarly-typed mooks to emulate each others' actions to provide cover fire or cooperate on actions, a very powerful effect!

    • When a generic mook (for example, a Booster Gangster or RatSec Exec) is placed in a scene as a part of a parent organization, they're also intended to inherent that organization's Gang/Corp-Wide ability, while still retaining the generic abilities that they always have!

  • Unique Abilities (shorthanded on mook sheets simply as Ability) are unique effects intended to provide particular mook sheets identity against each other. This way, a higher-ranking member of a gang doesn't just happen to have higher stats - they also get a new role within scenes!

    • These abilities are intended as a slimmed-down substitute for role skills, making mooks more than just target practice, without providing them overbearing power on their own.

Player characters do not have access to abilities for their own use - though they can be revealed at GM discretion through publicly-available knowledge, tactics/deduction checks, or sourcing information via the media or fixer role skill.

Items

The mook pack includes a wide variety of homebrew items. Some of these are simple - custom body armor for a particular SP, for example - but others can have unique gameplay effects that aren't otherwise available!

  • Unlike abilities, items should be made accessible to your players at any time they would normally be able to purchase such equipment.

  • The pack also includes a wide variety of items intended to improve player experiences, which may not even be used by mooks!

  • A complete list of all meaningfully distinct items are available within the NC G&C Items compendium.

    • It's recommended that you look over these before/during character creation with your players, so they understand the full array of materials available to them and the wider variety of playstyles that they enable.

New Items - Categories

A general (non-comprehensive) list of items the pack includes/introduces, and their purpose:

  • Ammunition - Status effects are lightly reworked for improved effectiveness, and several new/improved ammo types have also been introduced.

  • Armor - RAW, heavy armor types inflict brutal stat penalties when worn. The pack includes alternatives/substitutes where those penalties are removed, when certain BODY scores are reached.

  • Cyberware - Along with an array of misc. cyberware enhancements, the pack includes two notable cyberware expansions:

    • Feet of the Future - A rework/expansion of the available cyberleg options, bringing a layer of practicality and gameplay relevance to their usage through a modest series of buffs.

    • Skill Shards - A rework/substitute of the skill chips included in the core rules, buffing both their eligible use cases and reducing the overall amount of Humanity Loss inflicted across multiple chips to improve their appeal and viability.

  • Demolitionist's Cookbook - A series of items and materials designed to enable use of the Demolitions skill.

  • Netrunning Rebooted Materials - A suite of programs and gear for bringing netrunning actions into line with the remainder of the game, eliminating the need for detailed NET architectures and placing the associated actions alongside the remainder of those in the game.

  • Night City Runway - A collection of complete outfits with gameplay effects, designed to augment or outright replace the entirely cosmetic options available in the core rules and help your players live the cyberpunk mantra of "style over substance."

  • Vehicles/Transportation - Because vehicles from within the core rules cannot be reproduced/redistributed, a collection of lightly-modified substitutes have been included in the mook pack, as well as a considerable expansion of available options to include pickup trucks, buggies, and more.

    • The associated vehicles are also present as mook sheets within the actors compendium for use within scenes, with appropriately-sized tokens for use in play.

  • Melee Weapon Upgrades - A suite of upgrades applicable to melee weapons have also been included, allowing players to accentuate and customize their favorite melee toys.

Many of the mooks in the pack are implied to have cyberware; indeed, it wouldn't be Cyberpunk without it. However, unless cyberware has a functional purpose on the mook sheet, it has been omitted from the description to reduce bloat and save time.

Despite this, with only a few exceptions, almost every gang has chrome of some kind or other. In some cases, you can use it to explain a mook's Gang/Corp-Wide or Unique Abilities. In other cases, it may be purely cosmetic, or medical.

Microwaving Your Enemies

To save time and improve automation, a collection of Microwaver tables have been included within the NC G&C Rollable Tables compendium. These divided into three tiers: Basic, Elite, and Boss.

  • The @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsTables.RollTable.GG2mBcd2avqWMtPK]{Basic table} should be rolled any time your players make a microwaver/EMP attack against a mook of tier 3 or below.

  • The @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsTables.RollTable.vSEMSupxN0SJA4mb]{Elite table} should be rolled against tier 4 enemies, and circumstantially against tier 5 enemies.

  • The @UUID[Compendium.night-city-gang-and-corp-mook-pack.NCGangsnCorpsTables.RollTable.4J5ufZl8B7XkleMJ]{Boss table} should be rolled against tier 5 enemies, against anything you'd consider a serious cyberpsycho threat,

The higher tier the table, the worse the odds become of scoring "critical" effects, like a critical injury, and the higher the likelihood of scoring a "superficial" effect, which just lowers one of the mook's skills.

Scav, Much?

If your players ask, ultimately, it's up to you who in a scene has functional/salvageable cyberware, and how much - but a common theme among the gangs is the dehumanizing nature of their chrome. Consider producing substitute cyberware the has dramatically increased humanity loss, having likely changed hands (heh) 4 or 5 times prior to the user's installation and likely not well taken care of during that time, either.

Remember; if your players are yoinking from corpses, that should also cause humanity loss!

This pack uses a very simple framework for skills, as follows:

  • "Average" mook - stats @ 5-6, skills @ 9 total

  • "Elite" mook - stats @ 7-8, skills @ 10-13 total

  • "Boss" mook - stats @ 7=8+, skills @ 14+ total

Weapons use the Martial skill, which can be set via dropdown in the weapon's settings. Thrown weapons use physical. Any mooks which have weapons capable of such, need an autofire skill with the same rank as the martial skill; both are REF-based, so it should be the same number.

For reputation, I use the following guidelines, with rep reflecting:

Rep Bonus

Organization Type

+0

Unaffiliated individuals - random street thugs or the homeless, civilians, etc.

+2

Generic thugs and minor gangsters

+3

Most minor gangs which have clout, but which don't hold much territory

+4

Most major gangs and institutes - non-military corps, NCPD, 6th Street, etc.

+6

Military corps and hardcore gangs - Militech, Maelstrom, etc.

If you're interested in providing feedback on the pack's interactions or have suggested additions, let me know! I can be reached on reddit at /u/TheInvaderZim, and on Discord at prometheus.z or via the FVTT Cyberpunk RED server (link to invite).

Happy gaming!

The full contents of this pack would not have been possible without the assistance of some very talented individuals.

Generic Vehicles: Thanks to reddragon8928 on Discord for creating, donating, and allowing for the redistribution of his numerous vehicle tokens.

Non-Generic Vehicles, 6th Street Cover Emplacements: Thanks to Mattew Reger for his time and talents - find him on Instagram at kingof.roses (link).

Other Non-Generic Vehicles: Thanks to Solution for his work on some of the vehicle tokens, including RHIB boats and the Raffen vehicles. Find him on Patreon at SolutionMaps (link).

Custom Token Frames: Thanks to Charniecia Gardner for producing the token assets - find her on Instagram at runicwolfroxa (link).

Writing, Rules, Etc: All text included is based on characters and properties owned by R. Tal, and produced/distributed in accordance with their homebrew policy. All of it was written by me, Prometheus/Zim. No generative text programs were used in the creation of this work, though that's not to say I would take issue with their usage, if they were.

Token Art: All art from Midjourney, except where otherwise indicated on the mook sheet. The scope and volume of this project would not have been possible without AI assistance. Tokens were produced using the Rolladvantage Token Stamp webapp, and art sizes were optimized via theripper93's webapp. 5/5 solid programs, would recommend.

All included assets are redistributed with permission. Moreover, while I presumably retain some kind of rights to the pack itself, you're welcome to reuse, edit, and redistribute any assets included in it. I ask that you credit the artists involved when doing so, and myself, wherever the work appears. But it's the internet - expecting to retain control of your intellectual property after distribution is the height of hubris.

Please don't attempt to sell anything inside - not that I particularly care, but doing so is in violation of R. Tal's homebrew policy and they might get mad or something.

RED is intended to be a scarcity-based setting, where anything of real value is sold in shady backrooms or off the back of trucks by people who have connections, to people willing to pay the markup to access them.

Despite this, the Night Market tables included in the core rulebook are lackluster. In addition to poorly facilitating the DLC materials, many of the items included there are functionally useless or common enough that they could be purchased anywhere.

No longer!

If you stroll over to the NC G&C Rollable Tables compendium, you'll find a new array of updated night market tables have been included - 5 in total, one of each category of gear, fashion excepted. These tables:

  • Are made up exclusively of items worth 500eb or more - no more junk rolls!

  • Include most of the gear currently available within the core rules or DLCs, so long as that gear has a corresponding item within the Foundry RED system.

  • Include all of the items within the mook pack!

To use, simply attend a night market or set one up as a fixer, and start rolling! It's that easy.

(Note: night markets are flash sales and the items there go quickly - for maximum fun, have each of your party members roll on 2 tables, each time they attned a market.)
